content: A bone lesion with indistinct margins, wide zone of transition, aggressive periosteal reaction, permeative appearance, soft tissue component, or endosteal scalloping should be considered {{c1::aggressive}}.
A nonaggressive bone lesion is typically well circumscribed with {{c1::sclerotic margins}} and a {{c2::narrow zone of transition}}.
In an adult with a nonspecific aggressive bone lesion, the leading practical differential is {{c1::metastasis or myeloma}}.
In a child or teenager with a nonspecific aggressive bone lesion, the leading practical differential is {{c1::Ewing sarcoma or infection}}.
Periosteal reaction morphology helps estimate the {{c1::rate of growth}} and therefore the {{c2::aggressiveness}} of a bone lesion.

Most likely periosteal reaction pattern?

{{c1::Lamellated periosteal reaction}}

Most likely periosteal reaction pattern?

{{c1::Sunburst periosteal reaction}}

Most likely periosteal reaction pattern?

{{c1::Codman triangle}}
A slow-growing bone lesion can produce a {{c1::solid}} periosteal reaction because periosteal cells have time to lay down smooth continuous bone.
An irregularly growing bone lesion that grows in starts and stops produces a {{c1::lamellated}} or {{c1::onion-skin}} periosteal reaction.
A very fast-growing bone lesion can produce a {{c1::sunburst}} periosteal reaction with hair-on-end bone formation.
A lesion growing too fast for visible periosteal calcification except at the periphery produces a {{c1::Codman triangle}}.
A sharply marginated bone lesion with a thin zone of transition usually implies {{c1::slower growth}} and a {{c2::less aggressive}} lesion.
A bone lesion with a wide zone of transition suggests {{c1::rapid growth}} and a {{c2::more aggressive}} lesion.
In the Lodwick classification, geographic bone destruction with a thin sclerotic margin is {{c1::type 1A}} and is {{c2::almost always nonaggressive}}.
In the Lodwick classification, a moth-eaten pattern with no definable border is {{c1::type 2}} and is {{c2::aggressive}}.
In the Lodwick classification, multiple tiny holes infiltrating bone define the {{c1::type 3 permeative}} pattern.
A true permeative bone destruction pattern is very aggressive and is classically seen with {{c1::lymphoma, leukemia, and Ewing sarcoma}}.
Pseudopermeative bone appearance extending to the cortex suggests benign entities such as {{c1::osteoporosis or osseous hemangioma}}.
Osteoid tumor matrix, such as osteosarcoma matrix, appears as {{c1::fluffy cloud-like bone}}.
Chondroid tumor matrix, such as enchondroma or chondrosarcoma matrix, has a {{c1::ring-and-arc}} or {{c1::popcorn-like}} appearance.
A ground-glass bone matrix is classic for {{c1::fibrous dysplasia}}.
The fallen-fragment sign is seen with a {{c1::simple unicameral bone cyst}} after pathologic fracture.
An aneurysmal or expansile bone lesion suggests an {{c1::aneurysmal bone cyst}}.
Resorption of the distal clavicles or tumoral calcinosis can suggest {{c1::hyperparathyroidism}} with brown tumors.
In a patient over age 40, the two most likely aggressive lytic bone lesions are {{c1::metastasis and myeloma}}.
In a patient under age 20, an aggressive lytic bone lesion most likely represents {{c1::eosinophilic granuloma, infection, or Ewing sarcoma}}.
Eccentric bone lesions include {{c1::giant-cell tumor, chondroblastoma, aneurysmal bone cyst, and non-ossifying fibroma}}.
Central long-bone lesions include {{c1::simple bone cyst, enchondroma, and fibrous dysplasia}}.
